In this work, we prepared two series of nanocrystals: the AH-series which had only increasing amine content, and the OX-series which had a fixed carboxyl group loading with variable amine functionality. From these two series, we explored how their rheological characteristics, selfassembly, and application for film strengthening were affected by the changes in functionality. It was seen that both amines and carboxylate moieties imbue the nanocrystals with higher complex moduli, while at the same time, negating potential self-assembly characteristics with the emerging gel-like behaviour. Furthermore, surface functionality is an important factor in consideration of use as a physical reinforcement agent, as the OX-ChNCs negatively impacted the pectin-alginate film in terms of decreased tensile strength. Overall, it can be seen that the imparting of surface functionalities can greatly alter the properties and as well as the subsequent application of the asmodified nanocrystals. By tuning the chemical functionality of this nanomaterial, one can heavily distort its macroscopic properties such as its rheological and self-assembly characteristics.

query_text: How does the hindered nature of aryl iodide 7 contribute to its usefulness in mechanistic studies?
ground_truth_text: Aryl iodide 7 is a hindered substrate, and this gives it a special advantage in mechanistic studies. Instead of providing a single product, this substrate provides a defined mixture of three products 12, 13 and 3 that act as a signature of the radical process. (The chemistry is not confined to substrate 7; we will shortly disclose the reactivity of 8 and a range of other substrates).

retrieved.text: Aryl iodide 7 is a hindered substrate, and this gives it a special advantage in mechanistic studies. Instead of providing a single product, this substrate provides a defined mixture of three products 12, 13 and 3 that act as a signature of the radical process. (The chemistry is not confined to substrate 7; we will shortly disclose the reactivity of 8 and a range of other substrates).

Aryl radicals are intermediates in Base-induced Homolytic Aromatic Substitution (BHAS, Scheme 1). In these reactions, an aryl halide 1 couples with an arene 2 to form a biaryl. In detail, the substrate is converted into an aryl radical 4 that then adds to an arene 2 or heteroarene to afford radical 5. Deprotonation of 5 with KOtBu results in the formation of radical anion 6 which is transformed into coupled product 3 by transferring an electron to another molecule of the starting aryl halide 1, thereby establishing a BHAS cycle. BHAS reactions are generally efficient in producing high yields of products. The reactions are initiated by the formation of aryl radicals, usually with the help of an organic additive; a common mechanism of action is by reaction of the additive with alkali metal tert-butoxide to form an organic electron donor that initiates the BHAS cycle (i.e. converts substrate 1 to radical 4). However, regardless of the method of initiation, BHAS chemistry operates when aryl radicals are produced in the presence of arenes under basic conditions. Our recent report introduced substrate 7 as a useful mechanistic tool to identify BHAS chemistry and now, in papers related to this one, we show its ability to indicate when (i) Pd-catalysed reactions of aryl halides afford radicals in ground-state reactions and (ii) Ni complexes are involved in aryl radical formation. In this paper, we specifically provide a better understanding of the reactions of special substrate 7 by deuterium isotope studies.

To demonstrate the synthetic utility of this system, a variety of substrates without severe di-ortho-steric hindrance were now employed in the coupling reaction with benzene under our standard conditions (Scheme 3).

We reasoned that a monosubstituted arene bearing a particularly good leaving group (which could also function as a powerful electron-withdrawing group) could enable the formation of arynes using mild and functional groupcompatible conditions. The leaving group ability of onium salts from nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, and sulfur has been calculated to be the greatest for oxygen; 21 hence, we decided to examine whether oxonium salts could function as aryne precursors. Alkyl oxonium salts are exemplified by Meerwein's salt, which although kinetically stable, is a powerful alkylating agent that reacts with many nucleophiles, including water . It was not a result of abstraction from the side groups, since no other silicon containing products were observed in the reaction mixture.

Reactivity of aryl chloride in transition metal-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions is generally much lower than that of its aryl bromide/iodide counterpart, mainly due to the C-Cl bond reluctance to oxidative addition by Pd(0) . Developments of catalytic systems with Pd that are able to couple aryl chlorides were major achievements in organometallic catalysis in recent decades ; the pioneering studies with t Bu3P as an electron-rich and steric bulky ligand were initially reported in 1998 independently by Fu 124 and Nishiyama/Yamamoto . Further developments by design of new ligands with similar electron-rich and steric bulky feature quickly appeared in literatures , notably like biaryl ligands , NHC (N-heterocyclic carbene) ligands , ylidefunctionalized ligands and some of them could even activate aryl chlorides at room temperature or even below zero degree . Because aryl chlorides are much cheaper and available in large volumes worldwide, compared to their bromide/iodide counterparts. Their involved Pd-catalyzed coupling reactions are particularly attractive and were quickly utilized in the chemical/pharmaceutical industry .

Careful benchmarking of methods used in AA force field simulations has led to a reasonable understanding of the merits of the available models. Quantitative evaluation of accuracy is easiest for small (organic) compounds for which standard methods for force field parameterization are readily available and for which a large body of data is available from experiments, or from high quality quantum chemistry. Examples of such benchmarks are available for the gas phase, the liquid phase as well as the solid phase. These studies have established the root mean square errors for predictions from atomistic force fields to be around 7 kJ/mol for enthalpies of vaporization, around 3% for liquid densities, 66,67 8 kJ/mol for enthalpies of sublimation, 5% for solid densities and 40 K for the melting point. We recently extended this benchmarking work to a study of peptide crystals. In that study, we performed simulations of twelve peptide crystals using three modern atomistic force fields and found that some of the crystals deformed during long simulations (Table ). From this, it was concluded that simulations of organic crystals and peptides are challenging for atomistic force fields. Nevertheless, it may be possible to study amyloid peptide crystals and fibrils with suitable adaptations and refinement of atomistic force fields. Time scales for fibril formation form another hurdle on the road. Sarthak and colleagues attempted to explore the effects of point mutations on protein condensates, which have implications in neurodegenerative disorders, but even using the most powerful Anton-2 computer, 72 their simulations were unable to reach the time scales of required for fibril formation in vitro. Buell emphasizes that the thermodynamic aspects of fibril formation are an important topic for future amyloid research. Since aggregation of proteins and large peptides is slow, shorter peptides can still be useful to gain deeper understanding of the aggregation process. For instance, the potential of mean force calculations of crystal formation could help to shed light on amyloid thermodynamics, and such calculations should be tractable for all-atom models. Teijlingen et al. have investigated the self-assembly of short peptides using different Martini force fields (3/2.1/2.1P/2.2/2.2P). They highlighted the challenge in comparing results between Martini 2.1 and Martini 3 due to differences in bead types (side chain and backbone) and different Lennard-Jones terms for the same beads. One issue they reported is the overstabilization of "π-stacking" effect in Martini 2.1, yielding an energy minimum of -21.0 kJ/mol, compared to highlevel quantum chemistry calculations in the gas phase that give -7.5 to -11.7 kJ/mol.

Thus, calculations would suggest that the productive CMD step is irreversible and that the product distribution reflective of the difference in barriers between TS1-A and TS2-B. A simple H/D scrambling experiment (Figure ) demonstrated strong evidence that the CMD step is irreversible for this reaction. We anticipate that the deuterium incorporation in the -position is due to enol/enamine formation and deuteration by acid/base chemistry. Analysis of the competing transition states revealed two contributing factors resulting in this phenomenon (Figure ). First, an attractive π-π interaction between the arene and pyridone during may stabilize the preferred TS1-A. Secondly, steric repulsion between the phenylene moiety and pyridone ligand may disfavor TS1-B. In any case, this is a rare example of ligand control to effect linear methylene C-H activation in the presence of equidistant methyl C-H bonds. While previous reports have demonstrated that the CMD under similar conditions is reversible, this isotopic labelling study is consistent with accounts of mechanistic variations caused by small changes in substrate. Broadening this result to achieve general, ligand-controlled selectivity requires more experimental study and is ongoing in our lab. In conclusion, we have developed an efficient process for synthesizing benzocyclobutenes by Pd(II)-catalyzed methylene C(sp 3 )-H arylation with a transient directing group. This reaction is highly selective for forming four-membered BCBs by methylene arylation, and the intramolecular process outcompetes intermolecular reactivity. Due to the high selectivity for methylene arylation, an intact methyl group is available for subsequent C(sp 3 )-H arylation. Ongoing studies in our laboratory will reveal how these carbocycles derived in this way can lead to efficient syntheses of natural products and how to expand this result to induce a general methylene selectivity.

Benzyne 1 was first proposed as a reactive (diradical) intermediate by Bachman and Clarke during an investigation of the Wurtz-Fittig reaction 2 . Wittig later examined the phenyllithium-mediated elimination of fluorobenzenes and rationalized their observed reactivity in terms of a dipolar form of benzyne 3 . Roberts' classic C labelling experiment subsequently confirmed the existence of a neutral and symmetrical intermediate, most consistent with the bent acetylene-type benzyne structure now regarded as correct . Early experiments in the field of aryne chemistry were almost entirely focussed on mechanistic investigations until accessible and stable aryne precursors were formulated (Figure ) . Thermal decomposition of aryl ortho-diazonium carboxylates provides reliable access to arynes, but their shock sensitivity limits their broader application. The hexahydro Diels-Alder reaction of tethered triynes provides reagent-free (thermal) access to complex polycyclic systems, and transition metal-mediated functionalizations can be exploited as a mild and chemoselective approach to metal-bound arynes . Amongst contemporary precursors, Kobayashi's ortho-silyl triflate , is largely responsible for the rehabilitation of arynes as viable intermediates in synthetic chemistry as a consequence of its ability to generate a low steady state concentration of aryne upon treatment with a sparingly soluble fluoride source . This has also led to a resurgence in fluoride-free methods for aryne generation, often from monosubstituted precursors . Many of these methods use activated leaving groups such as l 3 -iodanes to enable milder conditions for aryne generation , but these often necessitate the use of strong bases such as LiHMDS or t-BuOK. There are relatively few reports of benzyne formation with weaker bases although notable progress has been made in the elimination of tricyclic l 3 -chloranes and bromanes .

However, such strategies cannot replicate the catalyst micro-environment resulting from CO2/HCO3 equilibrium, pH gradients, and equilibration of the electrode with CO as a product, highlighting the importance of directly interrogating CO2 reduction systems.

query_text: How does conditioning MS2Mol output with ground truth molecular formulas impact chemical structure prediction accuracy?
ground_truth_text: MS2Mol is designed to predict chemical structures without ground truth molecular formulas, as those are typically not known in discovery scenarios. However, the output of MS2Mol consists of molecular formula followed by chemical structure. This allows for the case in which the ground truth formula is known or can be accurately predicted. Since the output is generated autoregressively, prompting the output with the ground truth molecular formula allows the chemical structure to be partially conditioned on the correct formula, which increases accuracy. See Appendix D.

However, the output of MS2Mol consists of molecular formula followed by chemical structure. This allows for the case in which the ground truth formula is known or can be accurately predicted. Since the output is generated autoregressively, prompting the output with the ground truth molecular formula allows the chemical structure to be partially conditioned on the correct formula, which increases accuracy. See Appendix D.

In many cases, it is possible to accurately predict the molecular formula for a given MS/MS spectrum. MS2Mol can utilize this partial information by generating a SMILES string conditioned on both the MS/MS spectrum and the molecular formula. This functionality is possible due to our inclusion of the molecular formula tokens in the label sequence during training along with the autoregressive generation process of transformer models. Table shows the results of conditioning MS2Mol predictions on the true molecular formula. Such conditional generation leads to significantly more accurate predictions across the board. We emphasize that in this experiment, we are using the true molecular formula. In practice, one must first predict the molecular formula, and conditioning on inaccurate formula predictions can degrade the structure predictions.

To further investigate how the "darkness" of chemical space affects the performance of MS2Mol, we examine the performance of MS2Mol binned by the Tanimoto similarity between the ground truth structure and the closest molecule in the training set (excluding the simulated spectra). As with many machine learning systems, we observe that prediction accuracy is highly correlated with proximity of the unknowns to elements in the training set. The average similarity of predictions to ground truth is nearly 3x higher for spectra from the most similar (>=0.94 Tanimoto similarity) structures to train than for the least similar (between 0.44 and 0.50 Tanimoto Similarity). We further note a difference in performance across natural product classes as predicted by NP Classifier , with flavonoids, monoterpenoids, and steroids having the highest Tanimoto similarities, while macrolides, cyclic peptides, and fatty acids had the lowest. The latter result reinforces our observations regarding cyclic structures.

MS2Mol supports additional functionality that we do not evaluate in the main text. When the molecular formula is known at inference time, one can optionally require the beam search to output the correct molecular formula. Since each successive output token is conditioned on the previous outputs, this makes the structure prediction conditional on the given molecular formula and can improve predictive accuracy. Although we do not utilize this functionality in our main results, we do show the effect of formula-conditioned structure prediction in Section D of the Appendix. no_precursor_masking For this ablation, we set the masking probability of the precursor m/z tokens to 0, so that the model can always access these tokens during both training and inference. This can potentially lead to the model over-fitting to the precursor m/z or even memorizing which structure in the training set is associated with a given m/z. Such over-fitting is especially problematic when the test set contains novel compounds that have similar masses to compounds in the training set. Indeed, we see that this ablation performs uniformly worse than the baseline on our evaluation sets.

In addition to the above features, we also make use of a separate model from that accurately and directly predicts molecular properties (but not the full molecular structure) from the MS/MS spectrum. We emphasize that the property prediction model used here was trained solely on publicly and commercially available data, and never had access to internally-generated data. This property prediction model can often produce predictions quite close to the corresponding properties of the ground-truth molecule, and so it provides a valuable indication of the accuracy of the MS2Mol structure prediction. To utilize this fact, we include as features the difference and squared difference between the MS2Mol structure prediction value and the directly-predicted value, along with the raw MS2Mol structure prediction value, for the following properties:

In previous sections, we introduced the first evaluation of a de novo structure prediction technology on dark chemical space. As a baseline, we compared it to molecular and spectral library search intended to assign known structures to unknown spectra. In this section, we reverse the above scenario to ask how well MS2Mol performs on spectra from known molecules despite the fact that MS2Mol does not benefit from the reduction in search space afforded by limiting the search to structures contained in compound databases. This is a challenge setting, as the model is being evaluated outside of its intended use case.

Machine learning methods can close the gap between computational speed and the accuracy of atomic property predictions. There are several models available currently that can do this by evading the need for expensive QM calculations; instead, they rely on training to experimental data. Models such as SHIFTX2 , SPARTA+ 6 , UCBShift 7 , NMR GNN , and PROSHIFT are often used to support experimental chemical shift research for proteins. Specifically, SHIFTX2 integrates a sequence-based prediction model, SHIFTY+ with a structurebased neural network model, SHIFTX+, resulting in considerable accuracy for native protein states. By construction, it can falter when applied to non-native protein states due to the inability of the SHIFTY+ module to differentiate between folded and non-native states. The success of SHIFTY+ is also limited to whether the query protein has an aligned sequence match in its database. Consequently, for analyzing non-native structures or for sequences with no matched data, SHIFTX2's accuracy is contingent on its SHIFTX+ component. This has been recognized by work that has utilized SHIFTX2, where they note that although they use the SHIFTX2 program, the reported accuracy of SHIFTX+ is more reliable when considering their applications .

We further investigate MS2Mol by examining trends in the performance of predictions on EnvedaDark, noting potential strengths and weaknesses of MS2Mol in predicting natural products in dark chemical space. Firstly, we observe that MS2Mol is able to accurately predict structures even if molecules have fairly complicated extended structures with repeating subunits, as is relatively common for natural products; see Figure . When examining inaccurate predictions, we find spectra from "darker" chemical space, i.e. spectra whose structures are dissimilar to everything in the training set.

For many machine learning applications, particularly those in which poor predictions can be costly, it is desirable to not only have aggregate accuracy metrics but to also estimate the reliability of each prediction. This allows expert decision makers to segment predictions by confidence and potentially act only on those that pass a quality threshold. To enable this, in addition to the structure prediction model we trained a separate confidence model that predicts the Tanimoto similarity between the MS2Mol predicted structure to the ground truth structure.

On EnvedaDark, MS2Mol predicts a structure to within meaningful similarity of the actual molecule for 62% of spectra, and 21% to within close match, exceeding the highest database retrieval baselines by 44% and 95% relative accuracy, respectively at those thresholds. We evaluate MS2Mol's confidence model, which enriches the accuracy on meaningfully-similar and close-match thresholds to 98% and 63%, respectively, for the top 10% most confident predictions. We further evaluate MS2Mol on two sets of known molecules: CASMI-2022 and a novel set of spectra experimentally gathered from 454 known natural products, which we call EnvedaLight. We show that despite database methods excelling at retrieving exact matches, MS2Mol performs comparably to database matches at predicting meaningfully similar or close matched analogs even on known molecules. This introduces the possibility of using a single structure elucidation model to predict both known and unknown molecules in complex mixtures.

query_text: How does engineering the localized surface plasmon structure influence the antioxidation effect in plasmonic systems?
ground_truth_text: Extending our analysis to plasmonic systems, we find that LSPs on metal nanoparticles provide comparable or even greater photoprotection than cavities. Both dipolar and higher-order plasmonic modes contribute to this mechanism, with their relative importance depending on chromophorenanoparticle separation. Engineering the LSP structure, particularly the dissipation rate, optimizes the antioxidation effect by manipulating the emergence of a local minimum in the steady-state triplet population, which is dependent on the resonance frequency between the chromophore and the LSP field. Importantly, HEOM provides more accurate descriptions than BMME for these strongly coupled systems, especially when considering the tunability of the effective parameter range for photo-oxidation suppression.

retrieved.text: Extending our analysis to plasmonic systems, we find that LSPs on metal nanoparticles provide comparable or even greater photoprotection than cavities. Both dipolar and higher-order plasmonic modes contribute to this mechanism, with their relative importance depending on chromophorenanoparticle separation. Engineering the LSP structure, particularly the dissipation rate, optimizes the antioxidation effect by manipulating the emergence of a local minimum in the steady-state triplet population, which is dependent on the resonance frequency between the chromophore and the LSP field. Importantly, HEOM provides more accurate descriptions than BMME for these strongly coupled systems, especially when considering the tunability of the effective parameter range for photo-oxidation suppression.

In this work, we present a comprehensive study of photooxidation suppression in organic chromophores through engineered coupling to structured bosonic environments, specifically focusing on two possible implementations: optical cavities and localized surface plasmons (LSPs) . Using the hierarchical equations of motion (HEOM) approach to capture non-Markovian and non-perturbative effects , we demonstrate how environmental engineering can dramatically reduce triplet state population -a critical intermediate state in photo-oxidation pathways. We identify distinct mechanisms in both strong and weak cavity-chromophore coupling regimes: in the strong cavity-chromophore coupling regime, increasing cavity dissipation can enhance antioxidation through coherence-induced population transfer, while in the weak cavity-chromophore coupling regime, it can lead to a counterintuitive enhancement of triplet state population through a quantum Zeno-like effect. For practical applications involving LSP-chromophore systems, we show how engineering the environmental structure, particularly through optimization of dissipation rates and chromophore-MNP distances, can significantly extend the parameter range for effective photo-oxidation suppression. Our findings provide crucial design principles for protecting molecular systems while maintaining their functional properties, with important implications for quantum technologies and material science applications.

In addition to chromophores with ω S resonant with the dipole or higher-order multipolar modes, the selected chromophores with ω S = ω ′ 1,2,3 exhibit a local minimum in P TT at a specific γ p , as shown in Fig. ). This suggests that the antioxidant effect can be optimized by appropriately engineering the LSP dissipation.

Building upon our investigation of the single-mode cavitycoupled chromophore and its anti-oxidation behavior, we propose a more realistic model involving the coupling of chromophores to multiple-mode localized surface plasmons (LSPs) on metal nanoparticles (MNPs) as shown in Fig. . This approach offers several advantages over traditional cavity systems. The LSPs provide stronger field confinement and enhancement, leading to more robust light-matter interactions and enabling the exploration of both strong and ultrastrong coupling regimes . The multi-mode nature of LSPs allows for a broader spectral response, which can be advantageous for manipulating various molecular transitions . Importantly, the LSP dissipation rate can be tuned by engineering the environment structure, such as adjusting the substrate or surrounding medium of the nanoparticles . For example, employing high-index or absorptive substrates can increase LSP dissipation, whereas low-loss dielectric environments can decrease it . This tunability offers a powerful means to potentially control the anti-oxidation behavior of coupled chromophores by influencing energy transfer pathways and excited state dynamics through environment engineering.

The focusing of light into nanoscale hot-spot by nanogap plasmon results in a strong localized field enhancement effect, significantly boosting the light and matter interactions . This enhancement effect can amplify changes in optical processes when molecules are in proximity to plasmonic nanostructures, driving the development of plasmon-enhanced spectroscopy (PES) techniques including surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), tip-enhanced Raman spectroscopy(TERS), and surface-enhanced fluorescence (SEF), etc. These techniques provide rich information on the chemical composition, structure, and concentration of target substances, offering significant applications in studies related to reaction mechanisms, interface interactions, environmental detection, and molecular imaging research. Utilizing the progress in PES technology, significant advancements in achieving extreme sensitivity and ultra-high resolution have been made in recent years. For instance, Raman spectroscopy imaging, photo-induced fluorescence imaging, and single-molecule detection have been accomplished at the single-molecule level. Additionally, operating under ultralow temperature and ultrahigh vacuum conditions has enabled the resolution of individual molecular vibrational modes. Subsequently, sub-nanometer resolution Raman spectra of single molecules have been successfully obtained. The near-field distribution across the nanogap separation induces fluctuations in Raman signals attributed to the movement and diffusion of molecules within the plasmonic nanogap. These fluctuations pose critical challenges for the repeatability and quantitative detection of PES technology. Currently, some studies have been devoted to elucidating the intricate interactions between individual molecules and plasmonic nanocavities. A key focus of these studies involves unraveling the energy transfer between molecules and plasmon. Notably, the positioning of molecules within the nanocavity emerges as a critical factor significantly influencing energy transfer. The precision of high-resolution TERS signals is highly sensitive to the position of a tip with respect to a probed molecule. Furthermore, the molecular vibration can be strongly perturbed by changes in the distance between the tip and the molecule in the plasmonic nanocavity. Furthermore, the TERS signals can undergo substantial modifications based on the specific local environment of an individual zinc-phthalocyanine molecule. Despite the confirmed sensitivity of the interaction between molecules and plasmons, the mutual impact from the resonant single molecule within plasmonic nanocavities, particularly sub-nanometer cavities, and the local plasmonic near-field, remains inadequately understood. Additionally,most studies primarily focus on situations where the planar molecule lies down in the plasmonic nanocavity, and there is a notable gap in research concerning situations where a molecule adopts a vertical orientation parallel to the near-field direction.

The effectiveness of photobleaching suppression through cavity or plasmonic coupling critically depends on the engineering of the environmental structure. Recent theoretical work has shown that proper treatment of system-environment coupling, particularly in the strong and ultrastrong regimes, requires careful consideration of the environmental spectral density . The interplay between coupling strength, environmental structure, and dissipation can fundamentally alter molecular behavior , yet the potential of engineered dissipation for photobleaching suppression remains largely unexplored.

In the cavity-chromophore system, optimal antioxidation depends on the interplay between cavity-chromophore coupling strength and cavity dissipation rate. In the strong coupling regime, increasing cavity dissipation enhances antioxidation via coherence-induced population transfer, while in the weak coupling regime, it can counterintuitively increase triplet state population due to a quantum Zeno-like effect.

The plasmonic metal nanoparticles (PMNPs) support surface plasmon (SP), the collective oscillation of metal electron density. Under light irradiation with frequency that corresponds to the resonance energy of SP, the resonant photon induces a collective oscillation of conduction electrons of MNPs, then a phenomenon defined as localized SP resonance (LSPR) takes place. Because of the dramatic effect of LSPR, PMNPs have become an important component in many experimental settings, such as the surface-enhanced spectroscopy and plasmonic photocatalysts, in which PMNPs are used to control and manipulate light at the nanoscale, thus regulating the photophysical 1-6 and photochemical behaviors of molecules in their vicinity by means of the complex interplay between the plasmon and molecular quantum transitions. To unravel the detailed interplay between the plasmon and molecules, it is essential to computationally model and simulate the light-PMNP-molecule system.

query_text: How is the singularity of a chemical element quantitatively measured in the context of Substitution Formulae?
ground_truth_text: To measure similarity in this sense we use Subsitution Formulae (henceforth SF), e.g. X 2 SO 4 is a SF of K, Na, and whatever other elements can take the place of X. We denote the set of all SFs of element e as N(e). Note that an element has as many SFs as molecular formulae. SFs can be regarded as nodes in a bipartite graph where links exist between elements and their substitution formulae (Fig. ). Since SFs conform the neighborhoods of elements in this graph, we also refer to them as neighbors. Common neighbors establish similarity relations between elements. When a neighbor is linked to just one element we say that it is singular. We measured the singularity of an element as the ratio of its singular neighbors to its total number of neighbors.

Since SFs conform the neighborhoods of elements in this graph, we also refer to them as neighbors. Common neighbors establish similarity relations between elements. These shifts in singularity coincide with previously reported shifts in chemistry's growth regime, arising from the analysis of the heteroscedasticity of the curve of production of new compounds . The initial growth regime, named "proto-organic", is portrayed by its exploratory character, which is here again visible on the high frequency of formulae that are unique to elements during this period. The second regime, named "organic", covers the time period from 1860 to 1980; during this time, many new combinations were obtained and more similarity trends among the elements emerged, which reflects in the drop of their singularity. The third regime, which is marked by the growth of compounds involving organic chains and metals, and hence was named "organometallic" , coincides with the increase in singularity for the majority of elements.

Most of today's chemistry does not fit into any classification scheme based on stoichiometric patterns of molecular formulae, since unique combinations are the rule. 94.0% of the SFs are singular and encompass 77.9% of the compounds, and 42.2% of molecular formulae do not produce any similarity relation on any of its elements i.e. all their neighbors are singular. However, singularity is unequally distributed among elements, so that very high singularity is rare: 34 elements have singularity > 0.5 and only the 4 main bio-elements (C, H, O, N) are above 0.8. These four elements alone are responsible for more than 75% of the singular SFs. This is expected in a compound based similarity measure, given the complexity of organic molecules. Typical singularity is much lower: the average is 0.394, the median 0.412. So there are, in fact, numerous similarity patterns on the SFs of most elements, but also numerous singularities. Elements having more formulae tend to be more singular (see Fig. ), but there are many exceptions; even elements from the core such as I and Br may have a singularity around 0.5.

The number of IMFs in a search space can be calculated from the number of possible elements of a given type in the search space definition and the number of isotopes for that element. Equivalent duplicate combinations of isotopes must not be recounted to have a correct estimate, for example 12 C1 13 C1 is not distinct from 12 C1 13 C1 and a "missing" carbon. The allE variable is the set of all elements in the search space, and E is the current element being considered. MaxE is the max atom count of that element in the search space and k is the number of atoms of that element being considered at any one time. For elements with two stable isotopes, the sum becomes 2 0 + 2 1 + 2 2 … + 2 maxE , the product of each sum for each element is the number of possible IMFs in the search space. This enumeration includes formulas that are not chemically possible but nonetheless exist in the space.

The past decades have seen an increase in the production of singular compounds, which has caused elements to grow more dissimilar among them (Fig. ). In the early years of chemistry the bulk of elements had high singularity, as it is to be expected. Then, around 1860, their singularity started to decrease. This trend started to change after the 1950's, when the singularity of some elements (v.g. F, Mn) went into a steady increase for the first time in a century (Fig. ). By the 1980's this phenomenon has spread to 75% of the elements, including almost all those that were discovered before 1860. Hence, in the last few decades chemists have been pushing the boundaries of the chemical space towards particular chemical contexts of each element that produce unique formulae. Around 75% of the elements are growing in singularity during this period. The resulting trend on the similarity of elements is that they are growing apart, all the similarity patterns are waning into a growing solitude.

query_text: How does the miniaturization of functionalized graphene biosensors impact their application in diagnostics?
ground_truth_text: Miniaturization and Integration into Devices: Following functionalization, the next step involves integrating graphene into a biosensor device. This phase includes the miniaturization and design of electronic components that can efficiently interact with the functionalized graphene. The goal is to develop a device that is not only highly sensitive and accurate but also user-friendly and cost-effective. Miniaturization plays a crucial role in making these devices portable and suitable for point-of-care diagnostics.

retrieved.text: Miniaturization and Integration into Devices: Following functionalization, the next step involves integrating graphene into a biosensor device. This phase includes the miniaturization and design of electronic components that can efficiently interact with the functionalized graphene. The goal is to develop a device that is not only highly sensitive and accurate but also user-friendly and cost-effective. Miniaturization plays a crucial role in making these devices portable and suitable for point-of-care diagnostics.

The future of graphene-based biosensors in diagnostics is promising. Ongoing research is focused on enhancing their performance, expanding their applications, and addressing the current challenges in implementation. Innovations such as wireless connectivity for real-time data monitoring and the development of multiplexed biosensors capable of simultaneous detection of multiple biomarkers are on the horizon. As these advancements materialize, graphene-based biosensors are poised to play a pivotal role in transforming diagnostic methodologies and personalized medicine.

While graphene-based biosensors herald a new era in diagnostics with their unprecedented sensitivity and specificity, they face significant challenges in scalability, stability, biocompatibility, and regulatory compliance. Addressing these involves advancements in manufacturing processes for uniform, high-quality graphene, enhancing biosensor stability in biological environments, and ensuring seamless integration into existing healthcare systems. The future direction of these biosensors is promising, focusing on multiplexing capabilities for detecting multiple biomarkers, integration with IoT and AI for smarter health monitoring, and the development of wearable, flexible sensors. Overcoming these hurdles requires collaborative efforts across various disciplines, paving the way for graphene-based biosensors to transform healthcare by offering more personalized, efficient, and cost-effective diagnostic solutions.

Functionalization and Sensitivity Enhancement: A pivotal step in the development of graphene biosensors is their functionalization. This process involves modifying the surface of graphene to attach specific biological receptors such as antibodies, enzymes, or nucleic acids. Functionalization is crucial as it dictates the specificity of the biosensor towards particular biomarkers. Techniques like covalent bonding or -interactions are used to attach these biological entities to the graphene surface. The choice of receptor is dependent on the intended application of the biosensor, be it for detecting glucose in diabetes, specific proteins in cancer, or antibodies in infectious diseases.

Despite their potential, graphene-based biosensors face several challenges. One of the primary concerns is the reproducibility and scalability of graphene production, which is crucial for the widespread adoption of these sensors. Additionally, the functionalization of graphene, the process of adding specific molecules to its surface to enable the detection of particular biomarkers poses both technical and material challenges. Ensuring biocompatibility and minimizing potential toxicity are also critical considerations in the clinical application of these devices.

Furthermore, this paper addresses the challenges faced in the development and implementation of graphene-based biosensors. We explore the hurdles in material synthesis, functionalization techniques, and system integration, offering insights into the ongoing efforts to overcome these obstacles. By providing a comprehensive overview of the state-of-the-art in graphene bioelectronics and their applications in clinical diagnostics, this paper aims to shed light on how graphene is revolutionizing the field of biosensing, paving the way for advanced healthcare solutions that are both effective and accessible.

Advancements in nanotechnology may also lead to the development of new graphene composites or derivatives with enhanced properties, further improving the sensitivity and selectivity of biosensors. Another promising direction is the development of multiplexed biosensors capable of simultaneously detecting multiple biomarkers, which would be a significant step forward in diagnostic efficiency.

Nanotechnology's contribution to healthcare is multifaceted, offering novel solutions in drug delivery, therapeutic techniques, and, notably, in the development of highly sensitive diagnostic tools. Among these, biosensors represent a cutting-edge application of nanotechnology, redefining the limits of medical diagnostics. By integrating biological components with electronic systems, biosensors provide a direct and rapid means of detecting biological markers, making them invaluable in monitoring health status, diagnosing diseases, and evaluating treatment efficacy.

This review paper provides an extensive analysis of the current landscape and future prospects of graphene-based nano biosensors, with a focus on oral bioelectronics for salivary biomarker detection, we delve into the structural designs of graphene electronics and their applications in non-invasive health monitoring. Human saliva, easily accessible and rich in diagnostic potential, emerges as a promising medium for point-of-care diagnostics using graphene-based technologies. We discuss use cases of salivary biomarkers, evaluate the performance of existing graphene sensors in health monitoring, and highlight the innovative approaches that are shaping this field.

The electrical conductivity of graphene is pivotal in biosensor functionality. It facilitates efficient electron transfer, which is essential for the detection of low-concentration biomarkers, often required for the early detection of diseases. For instance, graphene-based biosensors have shown remarkable proficiency in detecting glucose levels, offering a boon for diabetes management. Additionally, the extensive surface area of graphene allows for a higher density of biomolecule attachment, improving the sensitivity and specificity of the biosensors. This feature is particularly beneficial in the detection of cancer markers, where precision is paramount. Graphene's mechanical strength and flexibility are also critical, especially in the development of wearable and portable biosensors. These properties enable the creation of robust biosensors that can endure physical stress, broadening their applications in various real-world scenarios. An example of this is the development of graphene-based wearable devices capable of monitoring cardiovascular health indicators, such as heart rate and blood pressure, in real-time.

query_text: How do excess electrons in the non-valence bound states of polyatomic molecular anions behave in oscillating electromagnetic fields?
ground_truth_text: In this regard, the quasi-free electron residing in the non-valence orbital associated with the polyatomic molecular anions is extremely attractive. As the nonvalence excess electron in the non-valence bound state (NBS) of the anion is quite loosely bound through the long-range monopole-multipole or correlation interaction , it is highly anticipated to behave like a free electron in the oscillating electromagnetic field. Sine there is only one state for the excess electron in the NBS most cases, the quantum defects caused by the electron-core interaction are expected to be non or insignificant. In this sense, the non-valence orbital in the various NBSs such as dipole-bound state (DBS) , quadruple-bound state (QBS) or correlationbound state (CBS) seems to be an ideal target for the investigation of the pondermotive effect on the polyatomic molecular anions. It should be emphasized that the intrinsic nature of the monopole-dipole (or quadruple) or correlation interaction is anticipated to be little influenced by the molecular complexity , meaning that the ponderomotive force could be effective even for the quite large polyatomic systems as long as the non-valence orbital survives during the oscillating electromagnetic field. It has been recently found that the DBS prepared below the detachment threshold, unlike the Rydberg states of polyatomic molecules, could survive quite long with the lifetime much longer than tens of nanoseconds or microseconds , although it should be noted yet that the NBS lifetime is subject to the detailed electronic structures in terms of the coupling between non-valence and valence orbitals . The NBS levels above the detachment threshold mainly decay by the rovibrational autodetachment process. According to our recent real-time dynamics studies on DBS and QBS , the Fermi-Golden rule provides the nice platform for the description of both absolute and relative autodetachment rates.

As the nonvalence excess electron in the non-valence bound state (NBS) of the anion is quite loosely bound through the long-range monopole-multipole or correlation interaction , it is highly anticipated to behave like a free electron in the oscillating electromagnetic field. Sine there is only one state for the excess electron in the NBS most cases, the quantum defects caused by the electron-core interaction are expected to be non or insignificant. In this sense, the non-valence orbital in the various NBSs such as dipole-bound state (DBS) , quadruple-bound state (QBS) or correlationbound state (CBS) seems to be an ideal target for the investigation of the pondermotive effect on the polyatomic molecular anions. It should be emphasized that the intrinsic nature of the monopole-dipole (or quadruple) or correlation interaction is anticipated to be little influenced by the molecular complexity , meaning that the ponderomotive force could be effective even for the quite large polyatomic systems as long as the non-valence orbital survives during the oscillating electromagnetic field. It has been recently found that the DBS prepared below the detachment threshold, unlike the Rydberg states of polyatomic molecules, could survive quite long with the lifetime much longer than tens of nanoseconds or microseconds , although it should be noted yet that the NBS lifetime is subject to the detailed electronic structures in terms of the coupling between non-valence and valence orbitals . The NBS levels above the detachment threshold mainly decay by the rovibrational autodetachment process. According to our recent real-time dynamics studies on DBS and QBS , the Fermi-Golden rule provides the nice platform for the description of both absolute and relative autodetachment rates.

Herein, we tackled three different molecular anions (phenoxide (PhO -), 4bromophenoxide (4-BP -), or 4-cyanophenoxide (4-CP -) to investigate the ponderomotive force on the excess quasi-free electron in the non-valence orbital of NBS. Quite remarkably, we found that all the non-valence bound states show the clear-cut ponderomotive effect induced by the non-resonant picosecond (ps) laser pulse at 791 nm. The overall behavior of the ponderomotive blue-shift (Δ𝜈𝜈 �) with the increase of the laser intensity (I) follows the free-electron model (vide infra). This already gives the important message that the large polyatomic molecular anions could be manipulated through the pondermotive force on the quasi-free electron in the non-valence orbitals. Intriguingly, whereas both DBS and QBS follow the free-electron model in terms of the linearity of Δ𝜈𝜈 � versus I, the QBS behaves more like a freeelectron than the DBS does. The model potential functions are found to be quite useful for the visualization of the diffuseness and associated polarizability of the excess electron in the non-valence orbital.

It has been found that the non-valence electron behaves more like a free electron in the QBS compared to that in the DBS, indicating that the non-valence orbital of the QBS is more diffusive and thus more polarizable upon the external oscillating electromagnetic field. Our finding here strongly promises that the chemicophysical properties of the polyatomic systems could be manipulated by utilizing the ponderomotive force on the quasi-free electron residing in the nonvalence bound states, paving a new way for the laser control of polyatomic molecules.

Since it was firstly conceived by Fermi and Teller in 1947, the dipole-bound state (DBS) of the anion has been both extensively and intensively investigated for many recent decades in terms of its structure and dynamic role as its study becomes increasingly important for understanding the whole anion chemistry and physics in many aspects. The lower limit of the dipole moment for the DBS formation had been initially predicted to be 1.627 D, although it seems to be now widely regarded (as a rule of thumb) that the excess electron could be attached to the neutral core when its dipole moment exceeds ~ 2.5 D. It should be emphasized though that the nature of the binding force between the excess electron and neutral core seems to be not straightforward as much as the literal meaning of the DBS may imply. Namely, the interaction potential is rather complex as all the possible monopole-multipole (such as dipole or quadruple) interactions as well as the correlation effect should contribute corroboratively to the binding dynamics of the excess electron to the neutral core though the extents of their contributions should vary depending on the particular chemical systems under various circumstances. In this regard, it may be fair to state that the DBS belongs to the category of the non-valence bound state (NBS) in a sense that its excess electron does not occupy any empty valence orbital whereas the more stable anion of which the excess electron resides in the valence orbital could be called the valence bound state (VBS).

Electron scattering experiments are complemented by photoelectron spectroscopy (PES) of nucleobase anions. These experiments demonstrate that during electron capture, a low energy electron may become associated with a nucleobase as either a dipole-bound (DB) or valence-bound (VB) anion, which are readily distinguished by their spectroscopic signals. DB anions can only form for a molecule with a sufficiently large dipole moment, as they are the result of association between an excess electron and a molecular dipole. DB anions are generally weakly bound, with a geometry relatively unperturbed relative to the neutral. An excess electron can also be captured by one of the unoccupied valence (usually anti-bonding) orbitals of the molecule, forming a VB anion. In nucleobases, the VB anion forms by population of the 𝜋 * orbital, causing a ring puckering distortion of the molecule. PES of uracil anions shows features from both DB and VB anions, with an intense, sharp feature at low binding energy, corresponding to a DB anion, as well as a broader feature associated with a rare tautomer valence anion. Photoelectron spectroscopy of the 4-thiouracil and dithiouracil anions by Bowen and coworkers, supported by computational work, demonstrates that both thiobases can form stable valence-bound anions in their canonical forms. These data show no evidence for stable dipolebound anions for either thiobase. However, DEA of 2TU implicates a DB state in facilitating dissociative electron attachment to the thiobase, indicating the possible importance of transient DB anions in these systems.

The ponderomotive force is the physical phenomenon experienced by the charged particle in the presence of the oscillating electromagnetic fields , which is extremely useful in the spatiotemporal manipulation of atoms/molecules/ions. The utilization of the ponderomotive force becomes more and more important not only in resolving scientific issues but also in the materialization of the cutting-edge modern quantum technology. As a trigger of the wiggling motion, the ponderomotive force has been vastly employed for the acceleration of electron/atom , aligning the atoms in an optical lattice , the high-harmonic generation , or the construction of the trapped-ion quantum simulators . The ponderomotive force shares the same origin with the dynamic Stark effect . The scientific merit of the dynamic Stark effect, though it is still quite rare, was demonstrated in terms of its active role in chemical reaction control as well as in the spectroscopic characterization of the ionization/detachment processes . And yet, it has mostly been confined to atoms or diatomic molecules only, and the dynamic Stark effect on the polyatomic molecular system seems to be still in infancy in terms of both the scientific understanding and its chemicophysical applications. From the molecular perspective, this is partially due to the fact that Rydberg states, which are expected to be largely influenced by the ponderomotive force , are extremely short-lived in the polyatomic molecules due to the strong and complicated interaction with the ionic core.

A substantial fraction of the remaining instances in which the oxidation states assigned by our model disagree with the ICSD occur for compounds that contain polyatomic ions. These may include covalently bound Zintl-type ions, or ions such as (SO4) 2-that can be thought of as a cluster of elemental cations and anions. Such polyatomic ions often have electronic structures, and hence boundaries between oxidation states, that differ significantly from those of their component atoms. For example, the sulfate anion appears to stabilize sulfur in a +6 oxidation state at potentials that are much more reducing than would be expected for an isolated sulfur ion, relative to the potential ranges for other ions in the material (Figure ). To account for this, we identified 23 common polyatomic ions based on their frequency of occurrence in the ICSD (Table ) and calculated oxidation state ranges for these as well. Our final model covers 84 elements and 23 polyatomic ions in 189 oxidation states (Table ).

Recent real-time pump-probe experimental studies on the state-specific dynamics of the cryogenically-cooled anions such as phenoxide and some of its analogues have revealed that the dynamic range of the DBS is quite wide especially in terms of its mode-specific lifetime. For the phenoxide anion, for instance, the DBS prepared below the photodetachment threshold decays quite slowly with its lifetime longer than tens of nanoseconds whereas the vibrational autodetachment of DBS above the detachment threshold takes place quite rapidly with a typical lifetime of tens of picoseconds. This experimental fact suggests that the DBS may undergo the rather slow radiative or nonradiative transition to the ground or excited state of the anion when the total energy is below the electron detachment threshold. On the other hand, when the internal energy given to the DBS gets higher than the detachment threshold, the excess electron is liberated very rapidly by the strong coupling between the electronic and nuclear motions.

Laser light of an appropriate wavelength and polarization (section 2.2.4) impinging on compound semiconductors yields nuclear spin polarizations of tens of percent. Dubbed "optical pumping" or "OPNMR," it has been the subject of many studies. At energies close to the band gap (i.e., the electron Bloch wavevector k equal to zero), the conduction band wave functions have orbital angular momentum L =0 and electron spin angular momentum S =1/2, for a total electron angular momentum J =1/2. The valence band wave functions have orbital angular momentum L = 1 and spin angular momentum S=1/2, for a total angular momentum J=3/2 or J=1/2. The J=1/2 states are "split-off" to lower energy (and are ignored here) so that circularly polarized light with helicity σ + tuned to the band gap (1.52 eV in GaAs) drives transitions from the mJ=-3/2 valence band state to the mS=-1/2 conduction band state and from the m J =-1/2 state to the m S =1/2 conduction band state, with relative probabilities of 3:1. Thus an excess of conduction electrons with ms=-1/2 is created. These photoexcited electrons may then be captured at defects, and nuclear spins proximate to the defect are polarized via an Overhauser cross-relaxation between nuclei and electrons. The rate constants associated with this Overhauser process 81 are affected by the detailed nature of the electron-nuclear couplings (Fermi contact, dipolar, indirect), which in turn may also be affected by the experimental design. 1030 Subsequent nuclear spin diffusion may carry this local polarization to bulk nuclei. The literature reports that this scheme for nuclear hyperpolarization may also be realized by manipulating the ensemble-averaged expectation value of the electron spin polarization via light modulation, 1030 application of fields, injection of electrons from magnetic materials, and dipolar order. In short, there are several ways to hyperpolarize electron and nuclear spins in these materials, setting the stage for potential quantum devices. GaAs has been a particularly fruitful venue for such demonstrations. Optical pumping in GaAs semiconductors is best illustrated by the "OPNMR profile" where the bulk NMR induction signal from, for example, Ga, is monitored as a function of optical excitation frequency and light helicity. "Spectra", or OPNMR profiles, represent the compilation of bulk NMR signal intensities on the ordinate and laser photon energy on the abscissa, as shown in Figure . The magnitude of these Ga NMR signals (as well as those of 75 As) approach tens of percent polarization, a significant hyperpolarization. These spectra are parsed qualitatively into three regimes based upon the energy of excitation photons relative to the optical band gap of the material: "sub-gap" (<1.505eV for GaAs), "mid-gap" (1.5-1.52eV), and "supergap" (>1.52eV). Sub-gap excitation leads to direct pumping to defect sites; mid-gap pumping yields mS=±1/2 conduction band electrons, depending on the polarization of light, where the electrons are subsequently captured at defects; super-gap pumping leads to free electron carriers with spatial trajectories determined by the applied magnetic fields of the NMR instrument. These features appear in the OPNMR profiles of many bulk compound semiconductors besides GaAs. Quantitative models for some aspects of these regimes are described in the literature; for example, elegant data 1033,1034 and a sophisticated model 1035 describe the supergap OPNMR profiles as variations in optical absorption due to the presence of Landau energy levels for the initial and final photo-absorption electron states. Midgap excitation, however, has been studied most frequently, as described below.

But the data attributes outlined above can make it difficult to choose a statistical method from the suite that is commonly applied, given the necessary assumptions. Often, a more flexible approach is required, and Bayesian methods are inherently flexible since there is no need to derive the sampling distribution of an estimator. Here, we propose a Bayesian generalized additive model for pipe loop study data that is sufficiently flexible for most pipe loop datasets. The collection of measurement series representing variation in a given parameter over time-often lead-is represented as the sum of five components (Figure ):

Pipe loop study data can be difficult to analyze, owing to non-linear time trends, seasonality, autocorrelation due to repeated measurements of pipe effluent, irregular sampling frequency, extreme particulate metals concentrations, and left-censoring, or values below the detection limit. To address these difficulties, we propose a robust Bayesian generalized additive model with continuous-time first-order autoregressive errors. With this approach, there is no need to impute non-detects, assume linearity, or treat outliers specially. Moreover, the model accounts for the reduced information content in autocorrelated observations. Our approach can be modified to suit the needs of diverse analyses. While time is the sole independent variable here, water quality parameters can easily be added as predictors if inferences concerning their effects are of interest. Moreover, any parameter in the likelihood can be modeled-including the standard deviation if model residuals are heteroskedastic. The model is equally useful for other problems featuring the partially-censored, irregularly-sampled, autocorrelated time series common in water quality analysis. But like many Bayesian models, this one is computationally-intensive to fit and would be difficult to implement in real time.

Given that pipe loop studies are expected to remain important for drinking water quality management, time series methods will be necessary for reliable statistical analysis of the data they generate. Methods that do not account for the effect of repeated measurements-and the reduced information content that results from autocorrelationare likely to be less useful, and may even be misleading. Methods should be flexible enough to account for nonlinear time trends, and they should be robust against extreme values. And since the goal of pipe loop studies is often to minimize the release of lead and other metals into drinking water, concentrations may fall below the analytical range for a given analyte. The most useful statistical methods, then, will be well-suited to modeling censored data.

Comparisons of corrosion control treatments are often aided by a statistical model, and typical choices in pipe loop studies include constant (e.g., t-test) and linear models. But when applied to an example time series from our dataset (Figure ), both of these models violate a key assumption: the independence of model residuals.

Pipe loop studies are used to model drinking water distribution systems, mainly for evaluating corrosion control treatment. The US EPA's Revised Lead and Copper Rule mandates them under certain conditions, and the data they generate will need to be analyzed. But pipe loop study data are inherently complex and there is a real risk of misinterpreting them. In particular, (1) non-linearity, (2) censoring, (3) autocorrelation, (4) irregular sampling frequency, and (5) outliers are difficult to model using standard approaches (Figure ).

Non-linear time trends and seasonal variation can be difficult to describe without overor under-fitting model parameters. Nonlinearity can also make it difficult to identify a stable phase, as typical guidance for analyzing pipe loop study data suggests. The most common models for pipe loop data are usually inadequate to describe complex nonlinear variation.

Censoring typically arises in pipe loop studies when analyte concentrations fall below the detection limit (i.e., left-censoring). Left-censoring is often addressed by substituting a constant for values below the detection limit and sometimes by removing the censored values. Neither approach is advisable as both introduce artificial patterns that can distort results, sometimes seriously. Popular statistical tests-for example, the paired t-test and its generalization, the one-way repeated measures ANOVA-cannot accommodate censoring. The non-parametric rank-sum test does allow for leftcensoring at one detection limit, but it is not adequate for more complex censoring schemes (e.g., multiple detection limits or interval censoring). Imputation methods such as regression on order statistics may be useful, but in a time series the censored data may be correlated, and imputation in the context of a full data model is usually preferable.

An advantage of our approach is the ability to move beyond point estimates and intervals: Bayesian models yield the full posterior distribution of all parameters and all quantities derived from those parameters, including the predicted values. This allows for propagation of uncertainty through all stages of post-processing and analysis. Moreover, the use of additive smoothing splines permits a description of time series using multiple nonlinear components, which can yield a more nuanced understanding of differences in analyte concentrations over time.

Notably, the sequence tensor representation does not natively retain invariance to sequence inversion as processed by our current RNN and CNN architectures. While the intermediate outputs of the CNN and pooling operations are equivariant to sequence inversions, the subsequent flattening operation and feeding into a DNN preserves the order of CU features. Thus, the final output is not invariant to sequence inversion. To address this, we take a two-fold approach when training models that takes a sequence tensor as input. First, we augment training data with inverted sequences labeled with the same property value as the forward sequence. Second, we average the output of the forward and reverse sequence to make predictions during testing. The former strategy acts as a form of regularization, whereas the latter ensures invariance to sequence inversion and can be seen as a type of test-time augmentation. Dataset B is further augmented with sequences constructed from cyclic permutations of the four CUs comprising the repeat pattern of the polymer, as previously described.

query_text: What machine learning approach was implemented to predict synthesizability of MOFs for cryogenic hydrogen storage?
ground_truth_text: To efficiently design MOFs suitable for cryogenic hydrogen storage, we down-selected synthesizable candidates for high-throughput screening instead of searching all candidates in materials databases. More specifically, we developed a ML model that can predict the synthesizability of MOF structures using the PU-CGCNN method, where CoRE MOF (experimental) and hMOF (hypothetical) databases were used as positive and unlabeled data, respectively. The CoRE MOF database consists of 12,020 experimental MOF structures, mostly derived from the Cambridge Structural Database, while the hMOF database includes 137,953 hypothetical MOF structures constructed from 102 organic ligands and various metal ions.

retrieved.text: To efficiently design MOFs suitable for cryogenic hydrogen storage, we down-selected synthesizable candidates for high-throughput screening instead of searching all candidates in materials databases. More specifically, we developed a ML model that can predict the synthesizability of MOF structures using the PU-CGCNN method, where CoRE MOF (experimental) and hMOF (hypothetical) databases were used as positive and unlabeled data, respectively. The CoRE MOF database consists of 12,020 experimental MOF structures, mostly derived from the Cambridge Structural Database, while the hMOF database includes 137,953 hypothetical MOF structures constructed from 102 organic ligands and various metal ions.

To conclude, we employ a machine-learning based model to and screened the MOF in the Experimental (CoRE) MOF database for their hydrogen storage performances and identify those that could potentially outperform high-pressure compressed hydrogen storage, even though some might not be originally designed for hydrogen storage. Extremely randomized decision tree model demonstrates the best performance across six machine learning methods, allowing accurate prediction of hydrogen uptake with less than 0.253 mg/g mean absolute error. Coupling these results with high-throughput system and techno-economics analyses, we show that for both backup power and industry decarbonization applications there are potentially 362 MOFs outperforming potential for most MOFs, despite the abundant models and experimental results available under cryogenic conditions. The LCOS under these conditions can be as low as $0.1/kWh to $0.3/kWh for operating under 45 cycles per year. From the analysis, we identify key features for promising MOFs as void fraction exceeding 0.6, a pore volume greater than 0.8 cm³/g, a density below 1 g/cm³, and GSA above 1500 m²/L. As the next steps, the feasibility from material development is needed, along with experimentally tuning MOF candidates, such as suitable materials with isosteric heat of adsorption within the range. Further, future research should focus on evaluating the degradation mechanism of MOFs under various applications scenarios and the impact of pressure regulation, as well as its associated hydrogen loss. Beyond improving the rigor and accuracy of future models, quantifying these influences at the system level will also be helpful to guide the deployment of hydrogen storage in real-world settings including power and industry decarbonization.

Among various physisorption materials, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are of great interest due to their porous structure, fast kinetics, and good cyclability . They have high porosity and enormous internal surface areas, which, together with their extraordinary degree of tunability and variability for both the organic and inorganic components of their structures , make them of great interest for gas storage. Over 10,000 MOFs structures have been compiled and analyzed in the Computational-Ready, Experimental (CoRE) MOF database, and this number continues to grow as new MOFs are continuously discovered and synthesized . Additionally, a substantial number of hypothetical MOFs have been predicted theoretically . This large collection of structures creates tremendous opportunities due to the chemical and physical diversity of MOFs, while also making it challenging to definitively characterize the potential role of material-based storage for specific hydrogen applications. High-throughput computational simulations have been employed to enhance the discovery and optimization of MOFs . Grand canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations have been used to calculate deliverable hydrogen capacity at 77 K by pressure swing . Furthermore, machine learning (ML) models have been used to predict the hydrogen capacity at 77 K with constant pressure , pressure swing and through pressure and temperature swings to 160K . These molecular simulations and ML predictions have identified MOFs with gas adsorption capacities predicted to exceed current state-of-the-art materials, and elucidated the relationship between material properties and hydrogen uptake. Despite these technological advances, most screening studies assume cryogenic temperatures (77 K) . While 77K temperature and/or pressure swing can achieve high hydrogen capacity, the refrigeration and corresponding infrastructure cost are substantial. For more economical and technically feasible applications, it is essential to understand the hydrogen storage capabilities of MOFs at mediumlow and near-ambient temperatures to develop commercially viable hydrogen storage solutions.

In this study, we presented a comprehensive material-to-system framework to screen, evaluate, and identify the cost performance of MOFs for hydrogen storage. However, there are some uncertainties and limitation in our current study. For example, we demonstrated the framework using the Experimental (CoRE) MOF database. And in the future, creating a broader screening database for MOFs that are primarily designed for hydrogen storage is beneficial, and would allow direct comparison between state-of-the-art MOFs for hydrogen storage. Second, it is important to acknowledge that although machine learning and high-throughput screening provides a general guidance to identifying the promising MOFs, the linkage between LCOS and material properties is not absolute, and there are intrinsic correlations between all material properties. . Future research should aim to develop a comprehensive screening methodology that quantifies the complex physical inter connections between the traits identified in this study, and their systemlevel impacts for ranking the MOFs.

Notably, we can see that our ML model can accurately predict the synthesizability of MOFs, since 99.85% of the experimental MOF structures are predicted to be synthesizable. On the contrary, we found that only 0.01% of the hypothetical MOF structures are predicted to be synthesizable (see Figure and). This allows us to focus more on synthesizable MOF structures by excluding over 100,000 non-synthesizable MOF structures. Interestingly, our ML model predicts that a few MOF structures in the hMOF database should be synthesizable, one of which was indeed synthesized (MIL-47(V)). This endorsement validates the excellent performance of our ML model in enumerating synthesizable MOF structures.

This study generated around 325,000 MOFs which were screened for post combustion carbon capture and two of those structures, namely Al-PMOF and Al-PyrMOF have also been synthesized. Another topology based MOF construction algorithm was developed by Gomez-Gualdron et al. and is called ToBaCCo. Hypothetical MOFs generated using ToBaCCo have been used for screening applications in hydrogen storage, methane storage and xenonkrypton separation. Some structures from this database were also synthesized and tested. Recently, a study by Lee et al. presented an algorithm to explore a MOF space of over 100 trillion materials, which was used to find the most optimal structures for methane storage.

The goal of the ML models was to predict excess hydrogen uptake for each of the three temperatures and six pressures. For ML training, we included features based on the work by Ahmed and Siegel 25 : temperature, pressure, and seven crystallographic properties of MOFs: pore volume, single crystal density, gravimetric surface area, volumetric surface area, void fraction, largest cavity diameter, and pore limiting diameter. We utilized various regression-based ML models to identify the optimal predictive model for our dataset. Among these, tree-based models, which use a branching method to make predictions based on feature values, forming a tree-like structure. The tree-based models we employed including decision trees (DT) , random forest (RF) , boosted decision trees (BDT) , random forest with bagging (B/RF) , decision tree with bagging (B/DT) , extremely randomized trees (ERT) . These models are known for their ability to handle complex data and make accurate predictions without feature scaling.

To address these knowledge gaps, we introduce a novel combination of molecular simulations, ML predictions and a TEA screening framework to evaluate the performance of various MOFs for different end users, as illustrated in Figure . In MOF-based hydrogen storage, hydrogen exists in two forms: excess uptake, which is the hydrogen adsorbed by the MOF, and gas-phase hydrogen present in the pores. The sum of these, measured at specific temperature and pressure, is known as absolute uptake. However, not all stored hydrogen is releasable in practical applications. The deliverable or usable hydrogen, which can be released through a pressure swing method, depends on the discharge pressure limit, typically ranging from 2 to 10 bar, a level sufficient to transport hydrogen through pipelines to end-users, such as fuel cell stacks or other industry applications .

In our approach, GCMC simulations and ML predictions are used to predict excess hydrogen uptake across a broad range of temperatures and pressures for all MOFs in the CoRE database that have been previously synthesized. These predicted data are then used to estimate usable hydrogen capacity (in effect, the amount of hydrogen released from storage at a target release pressure and temperature) under different conditions. Further, the usable hydrogen uptakes are incorporated into the TEA evaluation with material-level properties (density , void fraction , heat of adsorption (SI note 4), heat capacity (SI note 5) to calculate system-level cost of storage for two practical but distinct hydrogen applications: backup power and industrial heating. The levelized cost of storage (LCOS) for 9720 MOFs are determined and benchmarked against 350 bar compressed gas. The results of this work allow for the identification of MOFs with low LCOS for two critical applications of hydrogen, and highlight the optimal operating conditions and material properties crucial for economic performance. These insights into structure-performance relationships are vital for the development of new materials. The methodology developed in this study is applicable to evaluate MOFs in other database (such as CSD , ToBaCCo 18 etc.) and can be generalized to other hydrogen applications or gas storage scenarios.

The good performance of our ML model can also be visually identified using principal component analysis (PCA) and t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE). These techniques efficiently distinguish data points by visualizing high-dimensional data in two-or threedimensional maps. After examining the performance of our ML model for predicting synthesizability and identifying potential candidates, including recently synthesized MOFs not in the CoRE MOF database, we tested their performance for hydrogen storage. We then screened around 10,000 MOF structures without disorder using Vp and SBET criteria, as these two factors play a key role in H2 storage performance. As shown in Figure , we performed a three-step screening: (i) In the first step, we identified 1,316 synthesizable MOF structures with Vp values between 0.65 and 1.0 cm 3 /g. The reason for setting the upper limit of Vp is that the total volumetric H2 uptake at 77 K and 100 bar approaches the maximum value around 0.65 cm 3 /g, and the uptake slightly decreases when Vp exceeds 1.0 cm 3 /g (see Figure ). Moreover, the structural and mechanical stability of MOFs generally decreases with an increasing void fraction in the structure. Thus, we aimed to avoid extremely large Vp values.
